intro_train.title: The 22:47 Train
intro_train.narrator: Some notifications come at the wrong time. Or maybe the right one.
intro_train.text:
The train is almost full.

Weary faces.
Wet coats.
Headphones screwed in the ears.

Outside, the rain turns the windows into trembling mirrors.

You look at your reflection without really seeing it.

The speaker announces your stop in a voice too soft.

Then your phone vibrates.

Message.

One.

But your stomach gets tight before you even open it.

ignorer_message.title: Do not read
ignorer_message.narrator: We can repel a message. Not always what it contains.
ignorer_message.text:
You put the phone in front of your leg.

For three seconds, you're trying to be someone who doesn't look.

You thought it was over.
That it was better like that.

Then he's still vibrating.

Same sender.

Same silence around you.

Your stop is coming.

You know you should get up.

But your thumb already lit up the screen.

le_message.title: The Message
le_message.narrator: You didn't think a simple message could turn you around so much.
le_message.text:
The message is short.

> Before I left, I wanted to tell you...

You read the sentence again.

Once.

Twice.

You left three weeks ago.
Or maybe you left.
You don't really know who decided.

The world around you becomes more blurred.

You don't know what to feel: anger, sadness, relief, panic.

The train is slowing down.

Doors open.

A voice announces a station.

Not yours.

You're looking up.

Your stop is over.

train_rate.title: Too bad.
train_rate.narrator: Missing a stop is sometimes a general repetition.
train_rate.text:
Doors are starting to close.

You're not moving.

Then your body decides for you.

You get up too fast.

Someone's laughing.

Your bag is hitting a seat.

The door ringing speeds up your heart.

You're going to the last minute.

descente_precipitee.title: The Pier
descente_precipitee.narrator: There are seconds that contain all the weight of a life.
descente_precipitee.text:
You're coming down too fast.

The dock is wet.

The crowd is pushing you in every direction.

Your phone almost slips out of your hand.

You want to catch him.

Someone's shaking you.

Your foot is looking for the edge.

Don't find him.

A scream.

Metal noise.

A light.

Then nothing.

sur_les_rails.title: Always on the Rails
sur_les_rails.narrator: Silence after a fall never resembles silence before.
sur_les_rails.text:
You open your eyes.

You're still on track.

But the station is not the same.

No train.
No crowd.
No screams.

The neons illuminate the dock with cold light.
You hear the rain in the distance.

You're getting up slowly.

On all signs, the same time:

**22:47* *

Pier 1. Pier 4. Pier 18. Pier 52.

Always:

**22:47* *

A gray cat crosses the tracks in front of you.

He stops.

Look at you.

Then climb quietly on the dock.

appel_gare.title: No one answers
appel_gare.narrator: In some places, shouting is mainly used to hear his own fear.
appel_gare.text:
You call.

Your voice is lost under the windows.

Nobody answers.

Then the loudspeaker sizzles:

> Travellers arriving by accident are asked to reach their inner wharf.

You don't understand.

The mial cat.

Or maybe the sound comes from the speakers.

premier_quai.title: The Regrets station
premier_quai.narrator: All trains depart at the same time. All promise a different direction.
premier_quai.text:
The central hall is huge.

Lines extend as far as you can see.

Each panel displays:

**Departure: 22:47**

But destinations don't look like any city.

Line 03 — Life Rested
Line 11 — Successful Life
Line 18 — Children Rested
Line 27 — The Empty Pier
Line 34 — The Fatigued Train
Line 52 — Accelerated Life
Line 74 — Messages Never Sent

A woman sitting on a bench looks up at you.

**"First time? *

voyageuse.title: The Traveler Trempee
voyageuse.narrator: Some have been waiting so long that they have forgotten what they hoped for.
voyageuse.text:
The woman looks at the signs.

**"Here everyone is looking for the right train. *

You ask where they're going.

She laughs slowly.

**"Where we could have been happy. That's what we think at first. *

She shows you your phone.

The screen is black.

Then it lights itself.

The message is still there.

But one line has changed:

> I think we should have chosen ourselves sooner.
> Or maybe learn to choose yourself.

The traveller whispers:

**"The station likes to correct sentences. *

comment_partir.title: How to Leave
comment_partir.narrator: The simplest question is often the one that scares the most.
comment_partir.text:
**"How do I get out of here? *

The traveller is lifting her shoulders.

**"You can always get on a train. The problem is if we really want to get down. *

She's watching the cat.

**"Some people stay in a life. Others come back here again and again. Others are waiting for the last departure. *

**"What about you? *

She's smiling.

**"I wait to stop waiting. *

hub_gare.title: The Lines of 22:47
hub_gare.narrator: Each destination looks like an answer. None is really one.
hub_gare.text:
The panels change slowly.

The same lines come back.

The cat sits in front of you like he's waiting for you.

On your phone, the message remains open.

There's no network.

Yet the time always indicates:

**22:47* *

hub_gare_2.title: Other Lines
hub_gare_2.narrator: The more you explore, the more the destinations seem to know you.
hub_gare_2.text:
The panel rotates in a mechanical noise.

New destinations are emerging.

Line 34 — The Fatigued Train
Line 52 — Accelerated Life
Line 74 — Messages Never Sent

Everything goes at 22:47.

Always.

retour_gare.title: Return to 22:47
retour_gare.text:
The train is slowing down.

Doors open.

You're coming down.

The central hall is here.

Just like you left it.

The cat sleeps on an information panel.

The time has not moved.

**22:47* *

telephone_change.title: The Phone
telephone_change.text:
Your phone's on.

The message has been completed.

There was a suite.

> Before I left, I wanted to tell you...
> that it had nothing to do with you.
> Maybe I needed to disappear a little.
> Not from you. Just myself.
> I don't know if you understand.

You read the words one by one.

A little bit. *

What that meant.
What that might still mean.

You don't know.

You might never know if it was an end.
Or just a break.

The cat opens an eye.

Then the board of the lines sizzles.

ligne_03_intro.title: Line 03 — Life Rested
ligne_03_intro.narrator: Some trains do not go elsewhere, but to the place we have never left.
ligne_03_intro.text:
Train 03 smells of dust and old heating.

Through the window, you see your hometown.

Younger.
Quieter.
Goldier.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: the life you would have had while remaining.

vie_restee_1.title: The Natale City
vie_restee_1.narrator: There are cities we can't leave. Or that we never really bother to leave.
vie_restee_1.text:
The main street has hardly changed.

The bakery is still open.
Old movies show movies you forgot.
Someone calls you by your first name from a terrace.

You had an opportunity to leave.
A long time ago.
A job in another city.
A letter of acceptance you never sent.

You said next time.

There was no next time.

And it's not a tragedy.
That's just what happened.

choices.text: Go to coffee with old friends

vie_restee_2.title: Like all Thursdays
vie_restee_2.narrator: Habits can look like love. Or a very comfortable cage.
vie_restee_2.text:
The coffee is hot.

Conversations are easy.

We're talking about time, neighbors, work on the square, people who left.

When we mention those who left, there's a one-second silence.
Nobody's commenting on it.
Everyone noticed.

You look at your cup.

You could have been one of those people we're talking about with this silence.

vie_restee_seule.title: Too Known Streets
vie_restee_seule.narrator: You can get lost in a place you know too well.
vie_restee_seule.text:
You walk alone.

Every corner, a souvenir.

The first lie.
The first shame.
The first desire to leave.

This city didn't destroy you.

She kept you.

It's different.

On the platform of the small station, a train passes without stopping.

You don't know if you regret it or want it.

choices.text: Return to the Gare des Regrets

fin_vie_restee.title: End — Life Rested
fin_vie_restee.narrator: Some regrets don't make any noise.
fin_vie_restee.text:
You stay.

Time continues to pass.

Thursday coffee continues.
The streets keep their names.
Faces age at a reasonable speed.

One night, you find the acceptance letter.
Never sent.
The ink is still legible.

You put it in a drawer.

Not to remember.
To stop looking for her.

ligne_11_intro.title: Line 11 — Successful Life
ligne_11_intro.narrator: Some trains sell lights before talking about the price.
ligne_11_intro.text:
Train 11 is modern, brilliant, almost silent.

In the glass, your reflection changes.

Better clothes.
More confident look.
Phone filled with notifications.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: the life you have succeeded in.

reussie_1.title: The Scene
reussie_1.narrator: There's a version of you that said yes to everything we asked her.
reussie_1.text:
You hear your name chanted by a huge crowd.

Someone's making you look good.
Someone adjusts your jacket.
Someone tells you you're late.

You understand, after a few seconds, that this is your life.

That you wanted to.
That for that, you canceled dinner one night.
Then two.
Then a whole relationship.

That person said, "I can't keep going after your career." *

You said, "I understand." *

You lied.

reussie_2.title: The public
reussie_2.narrator: The crowd can fill a room and leave someone alone in the middle.
reussie_2.text:
The audience is screaming.

You know the songs almost by reflex.

Your body plays the role perfectly.

When the song ends, the applause goes through you without really reaching you.

Later, in the dressing room, some strangers congratulate you.

They know your name.

Not your silences.

reussie_loges.title: The Silent Lodge
reussie_loges.narrator: The room is full of gifts and flowers. Yet it seems empty.
reussie_loges.text:
You locked yourself in the dressing room.

The walls are covered with flowers.
Cards.
Trophies.

On a table, a phone displays hundreds of messages.

You're looking for a particular name.

He's not here.

He hasn't sent a message in three years.
Not since that dinner you canceled one last time.

fin_vie_reussie.title: End — Successful Life
fin_vie_reussie.narrator: Success is a form of loneliness that no one complains.
fin_vie_reussie.text:
You stay.

The rooms are filling up.
The lights are on.
People sing your name.

You learn to smile at the right time.
To respond quickly.
To thank sincerely.

Some nights, you look in the audience for a face you recognize.

Not a fan.
Someone.

He's never here.

But people applaud.
And that's something.

ligne_18_intro.title: Line 18 — Children Rested
ligne_18_intro.narrator: You never really go back to childhood. We only visit the memory we kept.
ligne_18_intro.text:
Train 18 has seats too small.

The windows are decorated with stickers.
An old bag is laid by the door.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: the time when dreams were not yet ashamed.

enfance_1.title: The Chamber
enfance_1.narrator: Somewhere, there's a version of you that never stopped drawing.
enfance_1.text:
You're in your nursery.

The walls are lower.
The brighter colors.
Toys exactly for them.

But you're an adult.

In the mirror, you have your face today.
In the bedroom, you have your hands before.

There's a date on a notebook.
The year you stopped.

Not because of a failure.
Not because of criticism.

You were afraid that wasn't enough.
So you chose something that was definitely enough.

It was a reasonable decision.

enfance_2.title: The Little Version
enfance_2.narrator: Children ask the questions we spend a lifetime avoiding.
enfance_2.text:
The child looks at you seriously.

**"Why did you stop? *

What are you asking?

She shows drawings.
Books.
Ideas of impossible worlds that you had intended to explore.

You could say, "I was scared."
Or I needed a stable salary.
Or: *nobody encouraged me at the right time*.

All three are true.
None is enough.

enfance_dessin.title: Drawing
enfance_dessin.narrator: Some things were there before we even understood them.
enfance_dessin.text:
The drawing shows a huge station.

All trains display the same time.

**22:47* *

You ask the child how she imagined this.

She's lifting her shoulders.

**"I didn't imagine it. I remember. *

The grey cat is drawn in a corner of the leaf.

choices.text: Return to the station with the drawing in mind

fin_enfance.title: End — Children Rested
fin_enfance.narrator: Some of the lives we give up continue without us.
fin_enfance.text:
You stay.

Days become simple.

Drawings.
Taste.
Rain against windows.
Stories before you sleep.

Nobody asks you to succeed.
Nobody counts your failures.

But sometimes, at night, you wonder what these impossible worlds would have become.

If someone had drawn them to the end.

ligne_27_intro.title: Line 27 — The Empty Pier
ligne_27_intro.narrator: Some destinations promise nothing. That's what makes them tempting.
ligne_27_intro.text:
Train 27 has no passengers.

Not even a driver.

The doors open without noise.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: the place where no one can ask you any more.

vide_1.title: The Empty City
vide_1.narrator: There are people who confuse silence with freedom.
vide_1.text:
The city looks normal.

Except there's no one here.

No cars.
No voice.
No birds.

The shops are open.
The red lights are working.

You wanted that once.
For a difficult time.
You thought, if only everyone would disappear.

You were ashamed of that thought immediately.

But she was back.
Several times.

vide_2.title: No request
vide_2.narrator: Being alone is sweet when you know that you can stop being.
vide_2.text:
You sit in a park.

The wind moves the trees.
A fountain flows.
The world is asking for nothing.

You could stay here.

No disappointment.
No misunderstanding.
No wait.

But you're starting to look for someone to show the fountain to.

And you understand something you already knew.

Silence is only pleasant when someone could break it.

vide_silhouette.title: The Silhouette
vide_silhouette.narrator: In empty worlds, you always find yourself.
vide_silhouette.text:
At the end of the avenue, someone walks.

You run.

The silhouette accelerates without ever turning around.

The closer you get, the more she looks like you.

Then she disappears in a subway mouth.

On the sign:

**Departure: 22:47**

fin_vide.title: End — The Empty Pier
fin_vide.narrator: We can choose absence. We don't choose what she takes.
fin_vide.text:
You stay.

The days don't really have a name anymore.

You eat anytime.
You sleep whenever you want.
Sometimes you scream just to hear something.

One morning, you realize you forgot how to start a sentence with someone else.

Not for lack of words.

Lack of practice.

ligne_34_intro.title: Line 34 — The Fatigued Train
ligne_34_intro.narrator: Some trains do not run on rails, but on exhaustion.
ligne_34_intro.text:
The train is crowded. But nobody talks.

Nobody's talking.

Everyone works: on computer, on phone, in his head.

Same rings.
Broken costumes.
Automatic smiles.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: life you never slowed down.

fatigues_1.title: The city that does not sleep
fatigues_1.narrator: There are people who have learned to confuse exhaustion with value.
fatigues_1.text:
The city shines all night.

The offices are full.
Coffees serve black drinks in nameless cups.
People walk fast, even those who have nowhere to go.

You said, at first, it's temporary.

That's what everyone thinks.
That's what everyone believes several years later.

You remember one night you could have refused.
One too many projects.
An impossible favor.

You said yes because saying no seemed a form of weakness to you.

That night, someone was waiting for you for dinner.

fatigues_2.title: Already Late For Tomorrow
fatigues_2.narrator: When everything is urgent, nothing really is anymore.
fatigues_2.text:
The floors look alike.

Meetings.
Messages.
Objectives.
Graphs.

Nobody screams.

It's worse.

Everyone smiled as if exhaustion was proof of commitment.

You forgot the date of birth of a close friend.
You missed your mother's birthday once.
You missed something small and irreplaceable.

You don't remember exactly what.

Maybe that's the most terrible thing.

fatigues_refus.title: Stop
fatigues_refus.narrator: In a crowd running, stopping looks like a rebellion.
fatigues_refus.text:
You stop.

People go around you without seeing you.

A man is shaking you.

**"Not now. *

You don't know if he's talking to you or talking to his own life.

The grey cat appears on a ticket dispenser.

He's yawning.

Behind him, an escalator descends to a station where all the clocks display 22:47.

choices.text: Follow the cat to the station

fin_fatigues.title: End — The Fatigued Train
fin_fatigues.narrator: We end up like the hours we spend.
fin_fatigues.text:
You stay.

Years become weeks.
Weeks become lists.
Lists become your way of breathing.

You make money.
You do things.

One night you find an old picture.
You look inside.

You wonder when you stopped looking like that person.

Not because of an event.
Because of the accumulation of all the small yess.

ligne_52_intro.title: Line 52 — Accelerated Life
ligne_52_intro.narrator: Some trains don't go anywhere else. They're moving too fast.
ligne_52_intro.text:
Train 52 trembles before leaving.

The ads are fast.
Doors are slamming.
The scenery is already rolling out while you're still at dock.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: life where you have never lost time.

acceleree_1.title: All Go Faster
acceleree_1.narrator: There are people who have learned to love speed so they don't have to stop.
acceleree_1.text:
People talk too fast.

Coffees are served before being ordered.
Children grow between two red lights.
The seasons change while we watch his phone.

You met someone who was doing everything slowly.
That took the time to finish his sentences.
He ordered his coffee and let it cool down.

You thought it was strange.

Then annoying.

Then you realized, too late, that it was a way of being present.

You've already moved on.

acceleree_2.title: Optimized Life
acceleree_2.narrator: Every minute won seems to claim an entire hour later.
acceleree_2.text:
You learn to answer faster.
Eating faster.
Choosing faster.
Forget it faster.

Years go through fragments.

A birthday.
A move.
A breakup.
A fresh start.

Everything goes so fast that nothing has time to leave a trace.

One morning, you don't know what city you spent Christmas in last year.

It's not freedom.
It's wear and tear.

acceleree_lenteur.title: Full Minute
acceleree_lenteur.narrator: Slowing down is sometimes more scary than running.
acceleree_lenteur.text:
You sit on a bench.

Nobody understands.

Passersby look at you like you stopped breathing.

Wait a minute.

A real one.

Long.

Imperfect.

You almost feel your heart taking over a human form.

Then the cat jumps near you.

fin_acceleree.title: End — Accelerated Life
fin_acceleree.narrator: Some lives look like a nice race to nowhere.
fin_acceleree.text:
You stay.

Everything's moving forward.

You do it fast.
You're changing fast.
You learn fast.
You lose fast.

One morning, looking at your reflection in a subway window, you don't remember your neighbor's name from the previous apartment.

Or the address.
Or the neighborhood.

You have some destinations left.
There's almost no place left for you.

ligne_74_intro.title: Line 74 — Messages Never Sent
ligne_74_intro.narrator: Some trains carry sentences that have never found a recipient.
ligne_74_intro.text:
Train 74 is quiet.

On the seats, off phones.
Closed envelopes.
Unnamed books.

The voice announced:

> Next stop: words kept too long.

messages_1.title: The City of Messages
messages_1.narrator: Things we haven't said continue to exist somewhere.
messages_1.text:
The streets are covered with messages.

SMS never sent.
Chewed letters.
Vocals suspended in the air like bubbles.

Some messages are sweet.
Some are excuses.
Some are declarations that arrived too late.

You're looking for yours.

You find one you started one night at the station.
Someone who might still be here.

You erased it.

You thought it was too late.
It wouldn't change anything.

That it was better not to say anything.

choices.text: Search for the message that brought you here

messages_2.title: The Original Message
messages_2.narrator: A sentence can change depending on who wears it.
messages_2.text:
You find the message.

It floats over a black fountain.

> I think we should have chosen ourselves sooner.

Then the sentence unfolds.

Versions of this message.
The one you wanted to send.
The one you were afraid to send.
The one that might have changed something.

Or nothing.

You'll never know.

That's the real weight of that message.
Not what he says.
What he doesn't know.

choices.text: Stay in this world of phrases

messages_autres.title: Words of Others
messages_autres.narrator: You think you're alone in your regrets until you read others'.
messages_autres.text:
You're reading.

Thousands of lives suspended in tiny sentences.

> I am proud of you.
> Come back.
> I don't blame you anymore.
> I should have called.
> I didn't know I loved you.

You cry without realizing it.

Not just for you.

For everything people keep in their throats hoping to have tomorrow.

messages_repondre.title: Answer
messages_repondre.narrator: Some answers are not meant to be sent.
messages_repondre.text:
You're writing.

Not for the other person.

Not really.

You write for the one you were on the train.

For the one who looked down at the wrong time.

> Maybe.
> But I'm tired of living in the lives I haven't chosen.

The message disappears.

Somewhere, a train is coming.

fin_messages.title: End — Messages Never Sent
fin_messages.narrator: Keeping others' words is a way of not letting them disappear.
fin_messages.text:
You stay.

You read everything.

Apologies.
Confessions.
Too late tenderness.

You become guardian of phrases that others have not been able to send.

Sometimes someone walks into the street looking for his message.
You show him where he is.

He's crying or laughing.
Rarely both at the same time.

You keep yours for the end.

For the day you know what to do with it.

ligne_chat_verrouillee.title: The Grey Cat
ligne_chat_verrouillee.narrator: Some guides do not follow at first.
ligne_chat_verrouillee.text:
You follow the cat.

He's crossing the hall.

Get past the lines.
In front of the travelers.
In front of the signs.

Then he stops in front of a dock without a number.

A black panel only displays:

**Line 88**

The cat is watching you.

Then the panel goes off.

Not yet.

ligne_88_intro.title: Line 88 — The Cat
ligne_88_intro.narrator: There's always a line that doesn't appear on any plane.
ligne_88_intro.text:
The cat walks through a dock you've never seen before.

No train.

Just the rails, the fog, and a clock stopped at 22:47.

Then the cat jumps on the tracks.

You want to scream.

But it doesn't fall.

He's walking on a vacuum.

Under his feet, images appear:
the hometown, the stage, the children's room, the empty town, the offices, the messages.

All lines.
Every life.
All absences.

coeur_gare.title: The Heart of the Station
coeur_gare.narrator: This place may not be here to answer. Maybe just to show.
coeur_gare.text:
You walk behind the cat.

The rails become a corridor of images.

You see yourself in every life.

The one that stayed.
The one that made it.
The one that never grew up.
The one who chose silence.
The one that ran too fast.
The one who lived among the messages.

None is false.

None is perfect.

The cat stops in front of a train door without destination.

On the door:

**Last departure — 22:47* *

dernier_depart.title: The Last Departure
dernier_depart.narrator: One day you have to choose without knowing what's on the other side.
dernier_depart.text:
The final dock is empty.

No destination.
No line panel.

Just a clock.

**22:47* *

The train arrives without noise.

Its windows are lit with warm light.

Almost real.

The cat sits in front of the doors.

The wet traveler appears on the other side of the dock.

**"You can stay in a life. You can keep looking. Or you can go upstairs. *

You ask where this train is going.

She's smiling.

**"No one comes back with a clear answer. *

final_train.title: Train Without Destination
final_train.narrator: Some doors open on an answer. Others on a softer question.
final_train.text:
You're going up.

The doors are closing.

The train starts.

For a while, there's only the sound of the rails.

Then other sounds appear.

Far away.

One voice.
Maybe your name.

A regular beep.
Maybe a machine.

Rain for a glass.
Maybe the station.

You look at your reflection.

For a second, you think you see your face hurt.

Then the reflection becomes normal again.

The cat sits on the seat opposite.

He slowly blinks.

The train is slowing down.

fin_ambigue.title: End — Doors open
fin_ambigue.narrator: Some ends don't say if we come back. They only ask what they would do if they did.
fin_ambigue.text:
Someone says your name.

Or maybe you can imagine it.

Doors open.

A white light enters the car.

You're taking a step.

In your hand, your phone vibrates one last time.

On screen:

> It's 22:48.

You're looking up.

And you move on.

fin_gare.title: End — Staying at 22:47
fin_gare.narrator: Not choosing is also a way of living, but the station does not call it that.
fin_gare.text:
You stay.

The trains continue to leave.

The wet traveler disappears one day.
Then maybe come back under another coat.

You learn the lines.
The announcements.
Newcomers.

You explain to others that they can look for the good life.

You know now she doesn't exist.

But you keep looking at the signs.

Because as long as all trains depart at 22:47, no possibility is really lost.

That's comforting.

It's a prison.

Both at once.
